coredata.dc:title: Table sugar derived carbon dot—A promising green reducing agent
coredata.dc:creator: V.A, Ansi
P, Sreelakshmi
Poovathinthodiyil, Raveendran
N.K, Renuka
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, carbon dots derived from Table sugar are used as a green reducing agent. Microwave assisted green synthesis is adopted for achieving bright cyan luminescent carbon dots from commercially available table sugar (sucrose). The particles are characterised by TEM analysis, XRD analysis, FTIR, XPS, Raman as well as photoluminescence spectral analyses. The system served as an environmentally benign reducing agent for Au(III) reduction. Gold nanoparticles of size ∼ 8.3 nm are achieved within seconds in presence of starch under mild heating. The system showed excellent stability for more than one month, and exhibited superior catalytic activity as evidenced by the hydrogenation of 4-nitro phenol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructures
Sugar derived carbon dots
Luminescence
Reducing agent
Nanogold

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Table sugar derived carbon dots
Carbon dots as green reducing agent
Catalytic activity of Gold nanoparticles - Reduction of 4-nitro phenol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterisation data of carbon dots. TEM image (a), particle size distribution - histogram profile (b), XRD pattern (c), FTIR spectrum (d). XPS profile- C1s (e) and O1s (f) spectra. Fluorescence emission curve under excitation Wavelength 360 nm (g) digital image of solution under UV excitation at 360 nm (h), Raman spectral profile (i) and DLS data for the measurement of Zeta potential (j).

Digital image of Au-NPs solution (a), TEM image of Au-NPs at various resolutions ; 200 nm (b), 50 nm (c) 5 nm (d) (inset of Fig. 2 d bearing TEM image of gold nano particle under 1 nm resolution), particle size distribution (e) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (f).
Catalytic hydrogenation of 4-nitro phenol to 4-amino Phenol using gold nanoparticles (Reaction temperature: 27 ⁰C).

head.ce:title: Table sugar derived carbon dot—A promising green reducing agent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala – 673 635, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, carbon dots derived from Table sugar are used as a green reducing agent. Microwave assisted green synthesis is adopted for achieving bright cyan luminescent carbon dots from commercially available table sugar (sucrose). The particles are characterised by TEM analysis, XRD analysis, FTIR, XPS, Raman as well as photoluminescence spectral analyses. The system served as an environmentally benign reducing agent for Au(III) reduction. Gold nanoparticles of size ∼ 8.3 nm are achieved within seconds in presence of starch under mild heating. The system showed excellent stability for more than one month, and exhibited superior catalytic activity as evidenced by the hydrogenation of 4-nitro phenol.

ce:section.ce:para: Implementation of green practices in chemical reactions, eliminating or least reducing the hazardous chemicals, has become a pressing necessity of high priority. Usage of safer alternative chemicals and more benign reaction pathways are always recommended for maintaining the green protocol [1]. There have been numerous efforts in this direction, by the substitution of every chemical reagent with an alternative, less hazardous one. Herein, we propose carbon dots as a green alternative for conventional reducing agents. Reduction is one of the most industrially significant chemical processes that paves way to an array of fine chemicals, which obviously demands large quantities of highly reactive reducing agents. Most of the reducing agents in the series often involve the use of expensive metals such as Pt and Pd, as well as toxic functional moieties, which initiated ceaseless efforts to green up the reduction process, including more effective and benign reducing agents which are also cost-effective.

Carbon dots, particles with size below 10 nm, are marked by a carbon core, crystalline or a bit amorphous, anchored with surface functional groups. It has been established that these nanoparticles have fascinating electronic properties, as evidenced by their spectroscopic attributes such as fluorescence, which bestow the system with fine applications in numerous fields, including sensing, biomedical field and catalysis [2–16]. Presence of surface oxygen functionalities tunes the system as an electron donor with potential applications as reducing agents. It will indeed be worthwhile to examine the use of these carbon dots as a novel class of environmentally friendly and economically viable reducing agents, which can at least replace some reagents in reduction processes, even if such applications are of limited scope. In this work, we demonstrate that table sugar (sucrose) derived carbon dots (TSCDs) can be used as an environmentally benign reducing agent in the synthesis of metal nanoparticles, and can be an effective substituent for traditional chemicals like NaBH4 and hydrazine. Significant attention has been there in recent years towards greening-up the metal nanoparticle synthesis, by replacing the above-mentioned reagents. Several alternatives like plant/animal origin extracts, living body cells, algae, fungi, natural polymers etc., have been examined to this effect [17–34].

The present work involves microwave assisted green synthesis of carbon dots, and the successful application in Au (III) reduction. Gold nanoparticles of average size 8.3 nm are yielded within a few seconds in presence of carbon dots and soluble starch under mild conditions. At this context, it is worthy to note that the precedented works available on the reaction of our interest using carbon dots involve harsh chemicals as well as significantly long reaction duration in the synthesis of carbon dots as well as the reduction process. Wang et al. have suggested a reaction path which involved large amount of sodium citrate, and took almost 4–8 hrs for the successful formation of gold nanoparticles with desirable size and features [35]. Though another group offered a reaction path devoid of capping agents, it strictly demanded the addition of concentrated NaOH solution and proceeds through stringent experimental procedures including overnight stirring and tedious post-chemical treatments for ensuring the purity of the prepared Au-NPs [36]. When compared to the previous efforts, the present approach for Au(III) reduction using table sugar derived carbon dots is of course greener in several aspects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Table sugar derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cyan emitting carbon nano dots were synthesised from commercially available table sugar (sucrose). Table sugar was subjected to caramelization followed by the addition of small amounts of diluted ammonia (90 % pure, Merck). The above solution was placed inside a micro wave reactor and was heated for 3 min at 120 ⁰C. The collected solution was further subjected to ultra-centrifugation for about 20 min at 15,000 rpm. The purity of the carbon dots thus obtained was ensured by conducting dialysis against distilled water using membrane with MWCO approximately 1 kDa.

HR-TEM image of carbon dots was achieved using Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope. RigakuMiniFlex-II diffractometer was adopted for X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the samples using Cu Kα1 radiation. Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR) spectrum of the sample was obtained via KBr disc method with the aid of JASCO FTIR - 4100 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with the aid of Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer using Al Ka X-ray beam. Fluorescent images were captured by placing the sample in LZC - 4X photoreactor. Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot was recorded with the aid of Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer. Quinine sulphate was used as the reference solution for measuring the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots. LabRam HR-Horiba Jobinyvon Spectrometer having Raman Microprobe with 532 nm Nd:YAG excitation source was used for recording the Raman spectrum. Dynamic light scattering studies was done using Anton paar Litesizer™ 500 light-scattering instrument.

In a typical procedure, 10 mL of distilled water was heated at 60° C for about 2 min, and 50 μL of 0.3 wt % starch solution was added as the capping agent with constant stirring. Subsequently, 25 μL of 0.05 M Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4.3H2O) solution was added by maintaining constant stirring. 20 μL of table sugar derived carbon dot solution was added to the reaction mixture, which turned wine red colour within 10 s, indicating the formation of gold nanoparticles (Au-NPs).

Formation of quasi spherical particles with average size 3−4 nm was confirmed from the HR-TEM analysis (Fig. 1 a), which was supported by the average size distribution profile of the system (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1 (c) shows the XRD pattern of carbon dots with characteristic peaks at 2θ values 24 and 44.5° corresponding respectively to (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon. The system was observed to be considerably amorphous in nature. The surface groups anchored on the carbon core were identified as hydroxyl, carbonyl and epoxy, from the FTIR spectrum, as evident from the characteristic bands at around 3400, 1700 and 1000 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 1 d). Further confirmation about these surface anchored groups was obtained from the binding energy values obtained from XPS profile of the system (Fig. 1e and 1 f). The peaks typical for C1s indicated the presence of carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, as well as CC bonds. As displayed in figure 1 g, the fluorescence emission curve of carbon dots showed a rather broad region of emission, with bright cyan luminescence under λexc 360 nm. Formation of bright cyan emitting carbon nanodots enriched with oxygen containing functionalities was confirmed through these results. The fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots was found to be 2.5 %. Raman spectral profile displayed both D and G bands, corresponding to in-plane and breathing vibrations, respectively, of CC bonds. Presence of highly disordered/defective carbon frame work (figure 1i), was indicated by the ID/IG value, 1.2. Dynamic light scattering study enabled determination of surface charge of the particles in solution [37]. As evident from figure 1 j, the zeta potential of the system was found to be -2.38 mV (pH = 5.5 maintaining room temperature).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots as green reducing agent
ce:section.ce:para: Oxygen containing functional groups of carbon dots bestow the system with excellent reducing character. The system served as an environmentally benign reducing agent for Au(III) reduction reaction. In presence of starch which acts as a capping agent, table sugar derived carbon dots could yield Au(0), under mild heating. Digital image showing wine red coloured solution of gold nanoparticles (Au-NPs) is displayed in Fig. 2 (a). TEM images of gold nanoparticles at various resolutions are presented in Figs. 2 (b), 2 (c) and 2 (d).

Average size of the particles estimated using the histogram plot of particle distribution (Image J software, version; 152-win-java8), was found to be ∼ 8.3 nm (Fig. 2 e). The characteristic surface plasmon absorption bands of gold nanoparticles was observed at 527 nm, as shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2 f)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of Gold nanoparticles - Reduction of 4-nitro phenol
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the catalytic action of the prepared gold nano particles, a model reaction i.e., hydrogenation of 4-nitro phenol was conducted using the system. About 1.7 mL 100 μM solution of nitro phenol solution was treated with 1 mL of NaBH4 solution for affecting the conversion of nitro phenol into nitrophenolate ion. The concentration of NaBH4 was maintained 100 fold excess than that of 4-nitro phenol so that pseudo-first-order kinetics for the catalysis was ensured. The above-mentioned conversion was indicated by the change in pale yellow coloured solution to bright yellow. Nitrophenolate ion shows characteristic absorbance at 400 nm, which remained unchanged till the addition of gold nano particle solution into it. With the introduction of nanogold, a gradual decrease in the absorbance at the said value was observed, denoting reduction of nitro phenol. Concomitant formation of a new peak around 298 nm was also noticed (Fig. 3 ), signifying the formation of 4-amino phenol, the desired product of hydrogenation of 4-nitro phenol, demonstrating the excellent catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles. The efficiency of the system was found to be comparable with other nanogold particles obtained using reducing agents of similar origin [38–40].

The reducing nature of table sugar derived carbon dots was illustrated in the study. Bright cyan luminescent carbon dots were synthesised from commercially available table sugar (sucrose) via Microwave assisted green synthesis. A facile synthesis strategy to gold nano particles via an environmentally benign route was presented, using table sugar derived carbon dots as the reducing agent, in presence of green capping agent, starch. Compared to the previous reports in this direction, the current reaction strategy yielded gold nanoparticles with fine features within a few seconds. Excellent catalytic activity of the gold nanoparticles was demonstrated by 4-nitro phenol hydrogenation reaction.
Ansi V.A: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Sreelakshmi P: Resources. Raveendran Poovathinthodiyil: Resources. Renuka N.K: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial support received from the Government of Kerala. Authors also thank Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut for providing the research facilities and STIC, CUSAT for providing the HR-TEM images.
